title: Sentry organization authentication tokens should not be disclosed
description: Sentry is an error tracking and application performance monitoring (APM) platform that helps developers identify, diagnose, and fix bugs and performance issues in real-time. Sentry organization authentication tokens (sntrys_ prefix) provide organization-level API access to Sentry's platform, granting programmatic control over organization settings, project management, team administration, release tracking, issue management, and alert configurations. These tokens enable comprehensive organizational operations including: creating and configuring projects across the organization, managing team members and their permissions, accessing and modifying organization-wide settings and billing information, retrieving sensitive error data with stack traces, user context, and environment variables from all projects, managing release deployments and associated source maps, configuring alert rules and notification integrations, accessing issue histories with personally identifiable information (PII) from error reports, managing API keys and authentication credentials, controlling integrations with third-party services (Slack, Jira, GitHub, PagerDuty), and accessing performance monitoring data including transaction traces and user sessions. Exposing Sentry organization authentication tokens enables attackers to: access comprehensive error data containing sensitive information such as API keys, database credentials, authentication tokens, and PII leaked through stack traces and context variables, manipulate or delete critical error tracking data to hide malicious activity or cause operational blindness, access release information revealing application architecture, deployment patterns, and potential vulnerabilities, steal source maps exposing proprietary code structure and business logic, modify alert configurations to suppress security incident notifications, access user session data including IP addresses, user agents, and behavioral patterns, exfiltrate performance monitoring data revealing system architecture and bottlenecks, manipulate team permissions to maintain persistent access or escalate privileges, access billing information and organizational metadata, modify integrations to intercept notifications or inject malicious webhooks, and delete or corrupt historical error data causing loss of critical debugging information and audit trails.
recommendation: Never hardcode Sentry organization authentication tokens in source code, configuration files, scripts, or version control repositories. Immediately revoke the exposed token from your Sentry organization settings (Organization Settings → Developer Settings → Auth Tokens → Revoke), generate a new replacement token with minimal required scopes (avoid 'org:admin' if possible, use specific scopes like 'project:read', 'event:read', 'releases:write'), and audit recent API activity in Sentry's audit logs for unauthorized access, data exfiltration, configuration changes, or suspicious queries. Store tokens securely using environment variables (SENTRY_AUTH_TOKEN, SENTRY_ORG_TOKEN), secret management systems (AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault, Azure Key Vault, GCP Secret Manager, GitHub Secrets), or encrypted configuration (never commit to Git, Docker images, CI/CD logs, or publicly accessible locations). Implement least privilege access by creating separate tokens with scoped permissions for different use cases: 'event:read' for error monitoring dashboards, 'project:write' for deployment automation, 'releases:admin' for release management CI/CD pipelines. Rotate organization tokens regularly (every 90 days recommended) and maintain an inventory of active tokens with their purposes and owners. Use Sentry's IP allowlisting feature to restrict token usage to known infrastructure IP addresses. Monitor Sentry audit logs for unexpected API calls, unauthorized project access, configuration modifications, or bulk data exports. Implement token expiration policies and automated rotation where possible. Review error data retention policies to minimize exposure of sensitive information in stack traces and context data. Enable two-factor authentication (2FA) on all Sentry accounts with organization access. Use Sentry's data scrubbing features to prevent sensitive data (credentials, PII) from being captured in error reports. Regularly audit organization member permissions and remove unnecessary access. Configure alert rules to detect unusual API activity patterns such as bulk data exports, rapid project enumeration, or unauthorized configuration changes.
impact: Critical security risk - Exposed Sentry organization authentication tokens grant comprehensive organization-level access enabling: sensitive data exfiltration from error reports containing API keys, database credentials, authentication tokens, and personally identifiable information (PII) leaked through stack traces and context variables across all projects; manipulation or deletion of error tracking data to conceal malicious activity or disable operational monitoring causing complete operational blindness; access to release information and source maps revealing proprietary code structure, application architecture, deployment patterns, and potential security vulnerabilities; theft of performance monitoring data exposing system architecture, API endpoints, database queries, and performance bottlenecks; modification of alert configurations to suppress security incident notifications enabling persistent undetected access; access to user session data including IP addresses, user agents, geographic locations, and behavioral patterns violating user privacy; manipulation of team permissions for privilege escalation or persistent backdoor access; access to organizational billing information and subscription details; modification of integrations to intercept sensitive notifications or inject malicious webhooks for lateral movement; corruption or deletion of historical error and performance data causing permanent loss of critical debugging information, compliance audit trails, and incident response evidence; and exposure of third-party integration credentials (Slack, Jira, GitHub, PagerDuty) stored in Sentry configurations leading to supply chain compromise.

compliant.description: Using environment variables for Sentry credentials
compliant.code:
import os
import sentry_sdk

# Load Sentry token from environment
SENTRY_ORG_TOKEN = os.environ.get('SENTRY_ORG_TOKEN')

sentry_sdk.init(auth_token=SENTRY_ORG_TOKEN)

checks.message: Sentry organization authentication token detected in source code. This token provides organization-level API access to Sentry's error tracking and application performance monitoring platform, granting comprehensive control over organization settings, project management, team administration, release tracking, and alert configurations. Exposing this authentication token allows attackers to: access sensitive error data from all organization projects including stack traces containing API keys, database credentials, authentication tokens, session cookies, and personally identifiable information (PII) such as email addresses, usernames, IP addresses, and user behavior patterns leaked through error context and custom tags, manipulate or permanently delete critical error tracking and performance monitoring data across all projects to hide malicious activity or cause complete operational blindness preventing incident detection and response, access detailed release information revealing application architecture, deployment patterns, version history, commit SHAs, and potential security vulnerabilities in production systems, steal source maps that expose proprietary code structure, business logic, API endpoints, internal function names, and intellectual property, modify alert rules and notification configurations to suppress security incident alerts enabling persistent undetected access and preventing security team notifications of suspicious activities, access comprehensive user session data including session replay recordings, user interactions, navigation patterns, form inputs, IP addresses, geolocation data, device information, and browser fingerprints violating user privacy and compliance regulations (GDPR, CCPA, HIPAA), exfiltrate performance monitoring data exposing system architecture, database queries with potential SQL injection points, API endpoint response times, third-party service dependencies, caching strategies, and infrastructure bottlenecks, manipulate team member permissions and roles to establish persistent backdoor access, escalate privileges to organization owner level, add malicious accounts, or lock out legitimate administrators, access organization billing information including payment methods, subscription details, usage patterns, and financial data, modify integrations with third-party services (Slack, Jira, GitHub, PagerDuty, Microsoft Teams) to intercept sensitive notifications, inject malicious webhooks for lateral movement, or steal additional credentials from integration configurations, delete or corrupt months or years of historical error data, performance traces, and audit logs causing permanent loss of critical debugging information, compliance evidence, incident investigation data, and forensic capabilities required for security incident response and regulatory audits, access and exfiltrate data scrubbing rules and sensitive data filtering configurations revealing what types of sensitive information the organization is attempting to protect, create malicious releases with backdoored source maps pointing to attacker-controlled servers to inject malicious JavaScript into error tracking UI when developers investigate issues, modify transaction sampling rates to hide malicious transaction patterns or cause performance monitoring blind spots, access environment-specific configurations revealing development, staging, and production infrastructure details, database connection strings, API endpoint URLs, feature flags, and security configurations, and enumerate all projects and their configurations to map the entire application portfolio identifying high-value targets for subsequent attacks. For organizations using Sentry for critical production monitoring, compromised tokens can result in: complete loss of operational visibility preventing detection of outages, security incidents, or performance degradations; theft of customer data and PII from error contexts violating privacy regulations with potential GDPR fines up to €20 million or 4% of global revenue, CCPA penalties up to $7,500 per violation, and HIPAA fines up to $1.5 million per violation category; exposure of authentication credentials and API keys leaked in stack traces enabling lateral movement to other systems and services; corruption of audit trails and forensic evidence required for security incident investigation and regulatory compliance; reputational damage from data breaches involving customer information captured in error reports; and loss of intellectual property through source map theft revealing proprietary algorithms, business logic, and competitive advantages. IMMEDIATELY revoke this token from Sentry organization settings at https://sentry.io/orgredirect/organizations/:orgslug/settings/auth-tokens/ (Organization Settings → Developer Settings → Auth Tokens → [Token Name] → Revoke), generate a new replacement token with minimal required scopes avoiding 'org:admin' or 'org:write' if possible (use specific scopes: 'project:read' for read-only project access, 'event:read' for error data access, 'event:write' for creating events, 'project:releases' for release management, 'org:read' for organization metadata, 'team:read' for team information, and avoid overly permissive scopes), audit Sentry's audit logs immediately for unauthorized API activity checking for: bulk data exports, rapid project enumeration, configuration modifications, unexpected API calls from unknown IP addresses or unusual geolocations, data scrubbing rule changes, integration modifications, team permission changes, and any suspicious patterns indicating reconnaissance or data exfiltration, review all projects for unauthorized changes to DSN keys, alert rules, data scrubbing configurations, release associations, and environment settings, check for newly created or modified integrations that could be used for data exfiltration or webhook-based attacks, examine recent error events for unusual patterns such as bulk deletions, artificial error injections, or tampering with issue resolution states, verify that no malicious source maps were uploaded that could inject JavaScript into the Sentry UI, review team member list for unauthorized accounts or suspicious permission escalations, check organization billing and subscription changes for unauthorized modifications, store authentication tokens securely using environment variables (SENTRY_AUTH_TOKEN, SENTRY_ORG_TOKEN never hardcoded), secret management systems (AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault, Azure Key Vault, GCP Secret Manager never in code repositories), or encrypted configuration files (never commit to Git, include in Docker images, log in CI/CD pipelines, or expose in publicly accessible locations such as public repositories, S3 buckets, or CDNs), implement least privilege access by creating separate tokens with scoped permissions for different use cases: 'event:read' + 'project:read' for monitoring dashboards and analytics, 'project:releases' for CI/CD release automation, 'event:admin' for error management tools, and 'org:read' for reporting systems (never use 'org:admin' or wildcard scopes unless absolutely necessary), rotate organization authentication tokens regularly every 90 days as a security best practice and maintain a documented inventory of all active tokens including their creation dates, assigned scopes, purposes, owners responsible for them, and last usage timestamps, enable and enforce Sentry's IP allowlisting feature to restrict token usage to known and trusted infrastructure IP addresses or CIDR ranges preventing usage from unexpected networks or locations, monitor Sentry audit logs continuously for suspicious activity patterns: unexpected API calls especially bulk operations, unauthorized project access or enumeration, configuration modifications particularly to alert rules or data scrubbing, bulk data exports or unusual query patterns, API calls from unexpected IP addresses, geolocations, or user agents, rapid successive API calls indicating automated scanning, permission changes or team modifications, integration changes particularly webhook additions, and token creation or modification events, implement automated token expiration policies where supported and establish procedures for regular token rotation cycles, configure comprehensive data scrubbing rules in Sentry to prevent sensitive information from being captured in error reports: scrub environment variables containing 'key', 'token', 'secret', 'password', 'api', 'auth'; remove query string parameters and request headers that might contain authentication data; mask personally identifiable information in user context, event tags, and breadcrumbs; and regularly audit scrubbing effectiveness, enable and enforce two-factor authentication (2FA) on all Sentry user accounts with organization access preventing account takeover even if passwords are compromised, regularly audit organization member permissions removing unnecessary access and following principle of least privilege: limit 'Owner' and 'Manager' roles to essential personnel, use 'Admin' role sparingly, prefer 'Member' role for most developers, and implement 'Billing' role separation for financial access, review and minimize error data retention periods to reduce exposure window of sensitive information captured in error reports balancing debugging needs with security and privacy concerns, configure alert rules to detect and notify on unusual API activity patterns: bulk data exports exceeding normal thresholds, rapid project enumeration suggesting reconnaissance, unauthorized configuration changes especially to security settings, API calls from unexpected geographic regions or IP addresses, token usage spikes indicating potential abuse, and error deletion patterns that might indicate evidence tampering, use Sentry's team-based access controls to segment project access ensuring tokens can only access projects within their authorized scope limiting blast radius of token compromise, implement webhook signature verification on all Sentry integrations to prevent malicious webhook injection and ensure webhook authenticity, regularly review Sentry's security advisories and platform updates ensuring your organization benefits from latest security enhancements and vulnerability patches, conduct periodic security audits of error data to identify and remediate instances where sensitive information is being captured despite scrubbing rules, maintain incident response procedures specifically for Sentry token compromise including: immediate revocation, audit log analysis, data breach assessment, affected customer notification if PII was exposed, and regulatory reporting if required by GDPR, CCPA, or HIPAA, use separate Sentry organizations for development, staging, and production environments preventing development token compromise from affecting production monitoring, and implement network segmentation and firewall rules to restrict Sentry API access to only authorized systems and networks preventing token usage even if compromised and exfiltrated from source code repositories.
